questions.question:
Throughout the excerpt, Sal watches the Winterbottom family interact and then reflects on what matters, ‘in the course of a lifetime.’

According to the last paragraph, how does watching the Winterbottom family help Sal understand the difference between things that matter and things that don't matter in life? Use evidence from the text to support your response.

responses.content: In the course of a lifetime. There are a lot that don’t matter. The text said Phoebe shouted at her mother. "You don't have to bake things for me. I'm too fat. And you don't have to wait here for me to come home. I'm thirteen now." which is pointless because no matter what her mother is still gonna be waiting for her. Second example is Mrs. Winterbottom stabbed the brownies with a knife. "Want one?" she asked. "They're burned," Phoebe said. "Besides, I'm fat." "Oh sweetie, you're not fat," Mrs. Winterbottom said. Which also Does not matter because her mother is going to try feeding her to Satisfy her. Last reason is  Phoebe said, "Are these cheerleading tryouts such a big deal? Will you even remember them in five years?" she thinks her mother will forget about something important about her

activities.text_title: Heroes Every Child Should Know: Perseus

questions.question: Look at paragraphs 46-47. How do Perseus's actions show that he followed Athene's plan?

responses.content: He grabs the head and carefully brings it back to the king while not looking at it
He wrapped the head in goat skin turning away the eyes and strands into the air aloft

questions.question: What does Perseus's response to the Gorgons in paragraph 49 reveal about his state of mind?

responses.content: Athene said “You shall take this polished shield," said Athene, "and when you come near her look not at her yourself, but at her image in the brass; so you may strike her safely. And when you have struck off her head, wrap it, with your face turned away, in the folds of the goatskin on which the shield hangs.” And he followed those rules exactly covered the head with the goat skin and slaying Medusa

questions.question: In paragraph 49, Perseus calls the sandals “brave.” What does this reveal about Perseus' character in this moment?

responses.content: Scared

questions.question: Based on Perseus's actions in paragraphs 46 through 47, the reader can conclude that—

responses.content: A. Athene will be pleased with the resolution of the plot's conflict.

questions.question: When Perseus's blood runs cold in paragraph 49, it reveals that he has—

responses.content: C. brief doubt in Athene's plan.

questions.question: Which phrase from paragraph 29 best signals the author's use of flashback?

responses.content: Percy is concerned he has to move schools
responses.bucket_rationale: The response accurately identifies Percy's concern as having to move schools, which matches the key scene elements in paragraphs 24-28. The answer is specific, text-based, and does not contradict the text. It meets both the form and accuracy requirements for the 'correct' bucket.

questions.question: How did the author structure the flashback in paragraphs 29-32?

responses.content: B. flood of memories came back to me

questions.question: How do paragraphs 30-31 support Percy's statement?

responses.content: The flashback is set backwards
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'The flashback is set backwards' shows some understanding of the structure, specifically that the memories go backward in time. However, it is vague and does not mention the trigger (mom's eyes), the specific order (third grade, then preschool), or the pattern of scary incidents. It does not contradict the text, but it lacks detail about how the flashback is structured. Therefore, it fits best in the 'partial-understanding' bucket.

responses.content: The memories are completely backwards
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'The memories are completely backwards' directly identifies a key structural element of the flashback: the memories are presented in reverse chronological order, starting with third grade and then moving to preschool. This matches the text, which does not present the memories in chronological order. The response is concise but accurate, and it demonstrates understanding of how the flashback is structured, not just what happens in it. The previous response also showed engagement with the idea of the flashback being set backwards, and the follow-up clarifies this understanding. Therefore, the response meets the criteria for 'correct.'

questions.question: Why might the author choose to use the flashbacks in paragraphs 30 and 31?

responses.content: D. to reinforce the idea that Percy is different and unique

questions.question:
Percy's conversation with his mother about his father serves as a turning point in the excerpt. How does this turning point propel the plot forward and hint at challenges Percy will face? 


Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, evidence, and explanation.

responses.content: Percy witnesses his mom crying and it gives him the fear of the unknown he found out his dad never met him. He does not know if it will be dangerous or not and want to return back to mom.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response describes Percy's feelings and what he learns (his father never met him, his mom is crying, he is scared of the unknown), but it does not analyze how this conversation propels the plot forward or what challenges it hints at. The answer focuses on emotions and summary, not on the analytical part of the question. This matches Score 1, Pattern E: Describes Emotions Only. There is no clear claim about plot movement or challenges, no evidence from the text, and no explanation connecting evidence to claim.

responses.content: Percy witnesses his mom crying. and it gives him the fear of the unknown. he found out his dad never met him. He does not know if it will be dangerous, or not. and he wants to return back to mom.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response describes Percy's emotions and what he learns in the conversation, but it does not analyze how this information propels the plot forward or what challenges it hints at. The answer focuses on feelings and facts, not on the significance for the story's direction. This matches Score 1, Pattern E: Describes Emotions Only, and Pattern A: Primary Unanswered.

responses.bucket_rationale: The response describes Percy's emotions and what he learns in the conversation, but it does not analyze how this information propels the plot forward or what challenges it hints at. responses.content: they gave her a motto you can

questions.question: Highlight ONE sentence or phrase that shows an example of when Margaret Bourke-White's hardworking attitude helped advance her career.

responses.content: Margaret felt happy
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'Margaret felt happy' expresses a positive feeling toward her work, which aligns with the text's depiction of Margaret as passionate and dedicated to her photojournalism. The answer is brief but meets the threshold for expressing a positive orientation, which is sufficient for a correct classification.

questions.question: Based on paragraphs 10-12, what can you infer was important to Margaret as a photojournalist?

responses.content: Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes. Her abilities resulted in some of the best steel factory pictures of that era, and these earned her national attention
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is: "Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes. Her abilities resulted in some of the best steel factory pictures of that era, and these earned her national attention". This is within the character limit and directly answers the question by showing how Margaret's hardworking attitude (solving a technical problem and getting national attention) helped her career. The highlight is also an exact match to an approved answer. Therefore, it is correct.

questions.question: Based on paragraph 14, how did Margaret influence future generations of women?

responses.content: complex events to the regular people staring social issues of the world
responses.bucket_rationale: The response identifies that Margaret wanted to make complex events and social issues understandable to regular people, which matches the key elements from paragraphs 10-12. The answer is text-specific, aligns with her actions and values, and does not contradict the text. The language is appropriate for a fourth grader and demonstrates understanding of what was important to Margaret as a photojournalist.

questions.question: Which of the following selections best states the central idea of this biography of Margaret Bourke-White?

responses.content: D. Margaret is a great example for women because she built a successful career in a mostly male profession.

questions.question: Which statement from the text most strongly supports the answer to the previous question?

responses.content: A. “She proved to women that they had every right to pursue the careers they wanted.”
